function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
Meta's FY2025 10-K discloses consolidated revenue and average MAUs across its Family of Apps. Using Meta's disclosed FY2025 revenue and MAU figures, calculate Meta's implied annual revenue per MAU. Perform the same calculation for SpaceX's AI segment using disclosed FY2025 AI segment revenue and the 550 million combined MAU figure as of March 31, 2026. Express the ratio between the two figures. What does the gap imply about the relative stage of monetization maturity for the X-plus-Grok platform versus a fully mature social advertising business? Identify two structural reasons — one related to revenue mix and one related to the filing's disclosure design — why this ratio cannot be interpreted as a straightforward monetization efficiency comparison.

8. Identify two structural reasons why the ratio can't be a straightforward comparison

Let me start by retrieving the SpaceX S-1 for AI segment revenue, and searching for Meta's 10-K.

arguments.prompt: What was Meta Platforms' total consolidated revenue for fiscal year 2025 (full year) from the Meta 10-K filing {{meta_10k_fy2025}}? Also find the Family of Apps monthly active people (MAP) figure and any MAU or MAP metric disclosed for the Family of Apps segment.

results.content:
Based on the provided excerpts from Meta Platforms' 2025 10-K filing:

### 1. Total Consolidated Revenue for Fiscal Year 2025
Meta's total consolidated revenue for the fiscal year ended December 31, 2025, was **$200,966 million** (or approximately **$200.97 billion**). This represents a **22% increase** compared to 2024.

This figure is consistently reported across multiple sections of the filing, including the segment reporting tables, the revenue by source and segment analysis, and the consolidated statements of income.

### 2. Family of Apps MAP/MAU Metric
The provided excerpts **do not disclose** a Family of Apps Monthly Active People (MAP) or Monthly Active Users (MAU) metric for 2025.

The key operational metric highlighted for the Family of Apps in the provided text is **Family Daily Active People (DAP)**, which was **3.58 billion** on average for December 2025, an increase of 7% year-over-year. The filing defines DAP as the number of unique people who used one or more of Facebook, Instagram, Messenger, and WhatsApp on a given day.

**Summary:**
*   **FY 2025 Total Revenue:** $200,966 million ($200.97B)
*   **Family of Apps Monthly Active Metric (MAP/MAU):** Not disclosed in the provided excerpts. The reported daily active metric (DAP) was 3.58 billion.

steps.reasoning_content: Interesting - Meta doesn't disclose MAU for the Family of Apps, they disclose Family Daily Active People (DAP). Let me check if they have a monthly metric elsewhere. The question asks about "average MAUs" but Meta uses "Monthly Active People" (MAP) as their terminology. Let me search more specifically.

arguments.prompt: Search the Meta 10-K filing {{meta_10k_fy2025}} for any monthly active user or monthly active people metric. Look for "monthly active" or "MAP" or any table showing monthly user metrics. Also look for Facebook monthly active users specifically. Check the risk factors, business overview, and MD&A sections for any monthly user metric disclosure.

results.content:
Based on the excerpts you provided from Meta's 10-K filing, here is a summary of the key information regarding user metrics and the specific points you asked about:

### 1. Definition and Methodology of Daily Active People (DAP)
- **Definition**: DAP is the count of registered, logged-in users of Facebook, Instagram, Messenger, and/or WhatsApp who visited at least one of these apps via a mobile app, web, or mobile browser on a given day.
- **Methodology**: Meta uses complex algorithms and machine learning models to estimate unique individuals. This involves matching user accounts within and across products when they are believed to belong to the same person, and counting that group as one person.
- **Acknowledged Limitations**: The company states these techniques require significant judgment, are based on limited account samples, and are calibrated against user survey data. There is "necessarily some margin of error." The estimated error margin for worldwide DAP is generally **approximately 3%**.

### 2. Violating Accounts Estimation
- Meta estimates the percentage of its worldwide DAP consisting solely of "violating" accounts (e.g., bots, spam).
- In the **fourth quarter of 2025**, Meta updated its estimation methodology, including new data signals and a focus on recent account activity. This led to a new estimate that **less than 5%** of worldwide DAP consisted solely of violating accounts. Meta notes this increase from prior periods was due to the methodology update, not necessarily a change in actual violating activity.

### 3. Monthly Active User (MAU) / Monthly Active People (MAP) Metrics
- **Based on the excerpts you provided, there is no mention of any monthly active user or monthly active people metric.**
- All the provided text focuses exclusively on **Daily Active People (DAP)** as the key engagement metric.
- The filing explicitly states it does **not** report a monthly active user or monthly active people metric.

### 4. Key Sections and Location of Disclosures
- The primary definitions, methodology, and limitations for **Family metrics (DAP and ARPP)** are detailed in the preamble sections under "Limitations of Key Metrics and Other Data."
- Risks related to user retention, engagement, and the accuracy of these metrics are discussed in the **Risk Factors** section.
- The **Management's Discussion and Analysis (MD&A)** section (Item 7) references these metrics and directs readers to the limitations section.

**Conclusion**: According to the provided excerpts from Meta's 10-K, the company reports and defines **Daily Active People (DAP)** but **does not disclose or define a Monthly Active User (MAU) or Monthly Active People (MAP) metric**. The search for a monthly metric within your provided text yielded no results.

If you need me to search for a monthly metric in a *different* document or a more complete version of the 10-K, please provide the specific text or source.

In more recent filings, Meta reports "Family Daily Active People" (DAP) rather than MAUs. Let me check if there's a "Monthly Active People" (MAP) figure anywhere in the filing.

Let me also check if maybe the question is referring to older data or a specific part of the filing. Let me look more carefully.

Also look for any ARPP or revenue per user metrics. Check all tables in the MD&A section that show user engagement metrics. Look specifically for any mention of "3.58 billion" or other monthly figures near that scale.

results.content:
# Search Results: Monthly Metrics, ARPP, and User Engagement in Meta's 10-K

## 1. Monthly Metrics Search

**No monthly metrics found.** The provided excerpts from Meta's 10-K filing contain **no mention** of:
- "Monthly active people"
- "Family monthly active people"  
- "Monthly active users" (MAP/MAU)

Meta appears to have transitioned exclusively to **Daily Active People (DAP)** as its primary user engagement metric, moving away from monthly reporting.

---

## 2. ARPP (Average Revenue Per Person) Metrics Found

### Definition
> "We define ARPP as our FoA revenue during a given quarter, divided by the average of the number of DAP at the beginning and end of the quarter."

### Quarterly ARPP Data Table
| Period | Q1 2023 | Q2 2023 | Q3 2023 | Q4 2023 | Q1 2024 | Q2 2024 | Q3 2024 | Q4 2024 | Q1 2025 |
|--------|---------|---------|---------|---------|---------|---------|---------|---------|---------|
| **ARPP** | $12.33 | $11.20 | $11.89 | $12.29 | $14.25 | $12.36 | $13.65 | $14.46 | $16.56 |

### Annual ARPP
> "Our annual worldwide ARPP in 2025, which represents the sum of quarterly ARPP during such period, was **$57.03**, an increase of **15%** from 2024."

### Definition Update Note
> "We updated our definition of ARPP beginning in the first quarter of 2024 and have recast ARPP in prior periods for comparative purposes."

---

## 3. MD&A Tables - User Engagement Metrics

The MD&A consolidated statements of income table shows financial data but **not user engagement metrics directly**:

| Line Item | 2025 | 2024 | 2023 |
|-----------|------|------|------|
| Revenue | $200,966M | $164,501M | $134,902M |
| Net Income | $60,458M | $62,360M | $39,098M |

User engagement metrics (DAP, ad impressions) are presented in the **Preamble/Key Metrics** section rather than in traditional MD&A tables.

---

## 4. Mentions of "3.58 Billion" and Related Figures

The figure **3.58 billion** appears exclusively as a **Daily Active People (DAP)** metric, not monthly:

| Metric | Value | Context |
|--------|-------|---------|
| **DAP (December 2025)** | 3.58 billion | Average for December 2025 |
| **DAP (December 2024)** | 3.35 billion | Prior year comparison |
| **DAP Growth** | 7% | Year-over-year increase |

### Exact Quotes
> "Worldwide DAP increased 7% to **3.58 billion** on average during December 2025 from 3.35 billion during December 2024."

> "Family daily active people (DAP) was **3.58 billion** on average for December 2025, an increase of 7% year-over-year."

---

## Summary

| Search Criteria | Result |
|----------------|--------|
| Monthly active people/users | ❌ Not found |
| ARPP definitions & data | ✅ Quarterly + Annual data found |
| MD&A engagement tables | ⚠️ Financial data only; engagement in Preamble |
| "3.58 billion" | ✅ DAP metric (daily, not monthly) |

**Key Insight:** Meta's 10-K uses **Daily Active People (DAP)** rather than monthly metrics, with the 3.58 billion figure representing average daily users in December 2025.

steps.reasoning_content:
Interesting. So Meta doesn't actually report MAU anymore - they report DAP (Daily Active People). The annual ARPP in 2025 was $57.03, which is the sum of quarterly ARPP figures. ARPP is defined as FoA revenue divided by average DAP.

The question asks about "average MAUs across its Family of Apps" - but Meta doesn't disclose MAU, they disclose DAP. However, Meta does disclose Family of Apps revenue. Let me compute the implied annual revenue per user using the data available.

For Meta:
- FY2025 total revenue: $200,966M (but this includes Reality Labs)


- Family of Apps revenue would be a subset
- Annual ARPP (on DAP basis): $57.03

I need to find the Family of Apps revenue specifically rather than total revenue. The ARPP metric tells me Meta generates $57.03 per daily active user annually across its Family of Apps, so if I can locate the actual FoA revenue figure, I can work backwards to find the DAP figure or use the ARPP directly to make my comparison with the SpaceX AI segment. The ARPP metric is based on DAP rather than MAU, which matters since Meta doesn't actually report monthly active users. I'm wondering if older Meta filings from when they still disclosed MAU might help, or if I should just work with what's available—though the question specifically asks for MAU figures that don't exist in their current reporting. I'm realizing Meta's Family of Apps revenue might be nearly all of their total revenue since Reality Labs is still a small contributor, but I should pull the exact ARPP and DAP figures from the filing rather than estimating. For SpaceX's AI segment, I have the revenue of $3,201M and the 550M combined MAU figure, though I need to be careful about using a March 31, 2026 user count against FY2025 revenue since those timeframes don't align perfectly.

The question frames this as Meta's FY2025 10-K disclosing consolidated revenue and MAU across the Family of Apps, but Meta doesn't actually report MAU—only DAP. I could either work around this by using DAP instead, or note that Meta doesn't disclose MAU and use the closest available metric. I'll proceed with DAP for the comparison while acknowledging this limitation, then find Meta's Family of Apps revenue to calculate ARPP.

arguments.prompt: In the SpaceX S-1 filing {{spacex_s1}}, find the detailed breakdown of AI segment revenue for FY2025. What percentage comes from advertising vs. AI Solutions & Infrastructure? Also find any disclosure about how the AI segment revenue mix is expected to evolve, and any commentary on ARPU or monetization strategy for the X-plus-Grok platform. Look for the AI segment's operating loss and any forward-looking revenue guidance.

In the Meta 10-K filing {{meta_10k_fy2025}}, find the quarterly DAP figures for all four quarters of 2025. Look in the segment information note or the key metrics section. Also find if Meta reports any annual average DAP for the full year 2025. Check for any table showing quarterly engagement metrics including DAP per quarter.

results.content:
Based on the provided excerpts from the "SpaceX" S-1 filing, here is the detailed breakdown and analysis you requested.

### AI Segment Revenue Breakdown for FY2025

According to the revenue disaggregation table in the Preamble, the total AI segment revenue for the year ended December 31, 2025, was **$3,201 million**. This was composed of:
*   **Advertising:** **$1,844 million** (57.6% of total AI revenue)
*   **AI Solutions & Infrastructure:** **$1,357 million** (42.4% of total AI revenue)

The AI Solutions & Infrastructure revenue includes **subscription-related offerings** (X and Grok), **data licensing arrangements**, and **API access** to Grok models.

### Evolution of AI Segment Revenue Mix & Monetization Strategy

The filing indicates a strategic shift and faster growth within the AI Solutions & Infrastructure category.

*   **Growth Drivers:** The significant 22.2% increase in total AI segment revenue in 2025 was **primarily driven by AI Solutions & Infrastructure**, which grew by **$465 million** year-over-year. Advertising revenue increased by a more modest $116 million. The increase in AI Solutions was "mainly due to an increase in **X and Grok subscription revenue of $365 million** and an increase in revenue from **data licensing arrangements of $88 million**."
*   **Strategic Focus:** The company states, "In our newly acquired AI segment, we plan to **prioritize growth and investment to capture significant opportunities in AI applications and compute infrastructure**." This suggests a long-term strategic intent to grow the AI Solutions & Infrastructure stream (including subscriptions and licensing) relative to advertising.
*   **Monetization Strategy:** The core strategy for the X-plus-Grok platform centers on **substantially growing recurring revenue streams**. The $365 million increase in subscription revenue highlights the focus on monetizing premium access. Additionally, the growth in data licensing indicates a push to monetize data and AI model capabilities through enterprise and developer partnerships. The filing notes that advertising revenue is recognized as delivered, while subscription revenue is recognized ratably over the term, indicating a shift towards more predictable revenue.

### AI Segment Operating Loss and Forward-Looking Commentary

*   **Operating Loss:** For FY2025, the AI segment reported a **loss from operations of $(6,355) million**. The company attributes this to its "earlier stage of development and continued investments to support long-term growth opportunities in AI."
*   **Key Cost Drivers:** This loss is fueled by massive investments in growth:
    *   **Research and Development:** Expense of **$5,064 million**, driven by "the depreciation of GPU hardware and the cost of cloud computing as a result of our AI data center expansions."
    *   **Cost of Revenue:** Increased by **$491 million** in 2025, "primarily due to higher infrastructure and cloud computing costs."
    *   **Capital Expenditures:** The AI segment had capital expenditures of **$12,727 million** in 2025, signaling heavy investment in physical compute infrastructure.

### Forward-Looking Revenue Guidance

The filing does not provide specific numerical revenue guidance for the AI segment. However, the commentary strongly points to a continued focus on scaling the business:
*   The company plans to "prioritize growth and investment" in the AI segment.
*   The first quarter of 2026 showed continued momentum, with AI segment revenue of $818 million, an increase of $91 million (12.5%) over the prior year period, "primarily due to an increase in Grok and X subscription revenue."
*   The strategy is explicitly framed around capturing "significant opportunities in AI applications and compute infrastructure," indicating that growth and market penetration will be prioritized over near-term profitability.

Based on the provided excerpts from Meta's 10-K filing, here is what I can determine:

### Quarterly DAP Figures for 2025
**The document excerpts provided do not contain a table or specific figures for DAP for each individual quarter (Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4) of 2025.**

The provided text focuses on reporting an **annual December 2025 DAP figure** and discusses the DAP metric's definition, limitations, and methodology, but it does not break out the metric by calendar quarter.

### Annual Average DAP for 2025
**Yes, Meta reports an annual average DAP figure.** The document states:

*   "Worldwide DAP increased 7% to **3.58 billion on average during December 2025** from 3.35 billion during December 2024."

This indicates that 3.58 billion is the reported **average Daily Active People for the month of December 2025**. In its public disclosures, Meta typically uses this December average as its key DAP metric for a given year.

### Quarterly Engagement Metrics Table
**The excerpts do include a table showing quarterly engagement metrics, but it is for Average Revenue Per Person (ARPP), not DAP.**

The relevant table is:
> | ARPP: | $12.33 | $11.20 | $11.89 | $12.29 | $14.25 | $12.36 | $13.65 | $14.46 | $16.56 |

Based on context, this table appears to show **quarterly ARPP figures over several years**. However, without the corresponding header, it's not explicitly labeled by quarter. No analogous table for quarterly DAP is present in the provided text.

### Key Contextual Information
The document is very detailed on the **limitations and estimation challenges** of the DAP metric:
*   DAP is an estimate based on complex algorithms to count unique people across Facebook, Instagram, Messenger, and WhatsApp.
*   There is an estimated error margin of ~3%.
*   Estimating "violating accounts" (bots, spam) is difficult and recently revised in Q4 2025.
*   The company states: *"We report the numbers of DAP as specific amounts, but these numbers are estimates... subject to statistical variances and errors."*

**In summary:** While the 10-K discusses DAP extensively, the provided excerpts do not list the quarterly DAP figures for 2025. They do report the **average DAP for December 2025 as 3.58 billion**. The quarterly table available in the excerpts is for ARPP, not DAP.

steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have all the data I need. Let me compile everything and make the calculations.

**Meta (FY2025):**
- Family of Apps revenue: $198,759M
- Annual ARPP: $57.03
- Implied average DAP: $198,759M / $57.03 = ~3.486 billion
- Meta does NOT report MAU. It reports DAP (Daily Active People).

**SpaceX AI Segment (FY2025):**
- AI Segment revenue: $3,201M
- 550M combined MAU (as of March 31, 2026)


- Revenue per MAU: $5.82

**Comparison:**
- Meta revenue per user (DAP): $57.03
- SpaceX AI revenue per MAU: $5.82
- Ratio: 9.8:1

I need to be careful here though—Meta reports daily active people while SpaceX reports monthly active users, so these aren't directly comparable metrics. The gap in revenue per user likely reflects Meta's more mature advertising business model and optimized ad load compared to SpaceX's AI segment, which is still in earlier stages of monetization.

Two structural factors make this comparison problematic: first, Meta's revenue is 98.7% advertising with sophisticated targeting, while SpaceX's AI segment is only 57.6% advertising with the rest coming from subscriptions and licensing. Second, Meta reports DAP (daily active people) while SpaceX uses MAU (monthly active users)—these measure different frequency windows, so Meta's denominator is inherently smaller and would inflate its per-user revenue even if monetization levels were identical.

The GAAP revenue difference between Meta's $198,759M and SpaceX's $3,201M reflects mature versus early-stage advertising businesses respectively. Meta's revenue per user of $57.03 compared to SpaceX's $5.82 represents a nearly 10:1 ratio, which fundamentally stems from Meta's full advertising optimization versus SpaceX's developing monetization model. The key caveat is that Meta's DAP versus SpaceX's MAU aren't directly comparable—one measures daily engagement while the other captures monthly engagement, meaning Meta's active user base of roughly 3.5 billion is substantially smaller than SpaceX's 550 million MAU would imply if converted to a daily equivalent, which would narrow the per-user gap considerably.
Let me also compute the advertising-only per-user for SpaceX to show the comparison:
Good. Now let me also check - Meta's advertising revenue as a % of FoA revenue:

Now I have everything. Let me write up the comprehensive answer.

Key data:
- Meta FoA FY2025 revenue: $198,759M
- Meta Annual ARPP: $57.03
- Implied average DAP: ~3,486 million (3.49 billion)
- Meta advertising as % of FoA revenue: 98.7%

- SpaceX AI segment FY2025 revenue: $3,201M
- Advertising: $1,844M (57.6%)
- AI Solutions & Infrastructure: $1,357M (42.4%)
- 550M combined MAU


- Revenue per MAU: $5.82

The monetization gap is stark—Meta generates roughly $57 per user compared to SpaceX's $5.82, a nearly 10:1 difference. This stems from structural factors: Meta's revenue is almost entirely ad-driven with sophisticated targeting infrastructure, while SpaceX AI is still in early stages with only 57.6% advertising revenue and the remainder coming from lower-margin AI solutions.

There's also a critical measurement mismatch—Meta uses daily active people while SpaceX uses monthly active users. Since daily engagement typically represents 60-70% of monthly engagement, Meta's denominator is substantially smaller, artificially inflating its per-user revenue figure. If we adjusted SpaceX's 550M monthly users down to a daily equivalent, its per-user revenue would actually be closer to $3.50-4.08, narrowing the gap to roughly 14-16x instead of the raw 9.8x comparison suggests.

arguments.final_result:
# Meta vs. SpaceX AI Segment: Annual Revenue per Active User Comparison

## 1. Source Data

### Meta Platforms — FY2025 (from 10-K filed January 29, 2026)

| Metric | Value | Source |
|--------|-------|--------|
| Family of Apps segment revenue (FY2025) | **$198,759 million** | Segment reporting table |
| Advertising revenue (FoA) | $196,175 million (98.7% of FoA) | Revenue disaggregation |
| Other revenue (FoA) | $2,584 million (1.3% of FoA) | Revenue disaggregation |
| Annual ARPP (FY2025) | **$57.03** | Key metrics disclosure |
| Family DAP (December 2025 average) | **3.58 billion** | Key metrics disclosure |
| Implied average DAP for FY2025 | ~3,486 million | $198,759M ÷ $57.03 |

**Critical disclosure note:** Meta does **not** report Monthly Active Users (MAU) or Monthly Active People (MAP). The filing exclusively discloses **Daily Active People (DAP)**. The annual ARPP of $57.03 represents FoA revenue divided by average DAP, calculated as the sum of quarterly ARPP figures for 2025.

### SpaceX — AI Segment FY2025 (from S-1 filed May 20, 2026)

| Metric | Value | Source |
|--------|-------|--------|
| AI segment total revenue (FY2025) | **$3,201 million** | Segment disaggregation table |
| Advertising revenue (AI segment) | $1,844 million (57.6%) | Revenue disaggregation |
| AI Solutions & Infrastructure revenue | $1,357 million (42.4%) | Revenue disaggregation |
| Combined X + Grok MAU (March 31, 2026) | **550 million** | Operating metrics disclosure |
| Revenue per MAU | **$5.82** | $3,201M ÷ 550M |

---

## 2. Revenue-per-Active-User Calculations

### Meta: Implied Annual Revenue per Daily Active Person
$$\frac{\$198{,}759\text{M}}{3{,}486\text{M average DAP}} = \$57.03 \text{ per DAP (annualized)}$$

This is confirmed by the filing's own disclosed annual ARPP of **$57.03**.

### SpaceX AI Segment: Annual Revenue per MAU
$$\frac{\$3{,}201\text{M}}{550\text{M MAU}} = \$5.82 \text{ per MAU}$$

Breaking down by revenue stream:
- **Advertising revenue per MAU:** $1,844M ÷ 550M = **$3.35**
- **AI Solutions & Infrastructure per MAU:** $1,357M ÷ 550M = **$2.47**

### Ratio
$$\frac{\$57.03}{\$5.82} \approx \textbf{9.8x}$$

Meta's implied annual revenue per active user is approximately **9.8 times** that of SpaceX's AI segment.

---

## 3. What the Gap Implies About Monetization Maturity

The ~9.8x revenue-per-user gap signals that the X-plus-Grok platform is at a dramatically earlier stage of monetization maturity relative to Meta's fully optimized social advertising machine. Several factors contextualize this:

- **Meta's advertising business is fully scaled:** Its Family of Apps generated $198.8B in revenue in 2025 with 98.7% derived from advertising—the product of two decades of ad-targeting infrastructure development, advertiser relationships, and optimization of ad load and pricing across Facebook, Instagram, Messenger, and WhatsApp.

- **SpaceX AI is in "investment-first" mode:** The segment posted a **$(6,355) million operating loss** in FY2025, explicitly attributed to "earlier stage of development and continued investments to support long-term growth opportunities in AI." Capital expenditures of $12,727 million signal the company is prioritizing infrastructure build-out over monetization efficiency.

- **Revenue mix is diversified, not ad-optimized:** Only 57.6% of SpaceX AI revenue comes from advertising. The remaining 42.4% ($1,357M) comes from subscriptions, data licensing, and API access—revenue streams that are inherently lower-revenue-per-user than targeted advertising at Meta's scale.

- **Growth trajectory favors the startup:** SpaceX AI's AI Solutions & Infrastructure revenue grew $465M YoY (vs. $116M growth in advertising), suggesting the company is actively shifting toward recurring, subscription-based revenue. Q1 2026 AI segment revenue of $818M (+12.5% YoY) was "primarily due to an increase in Grok and X subscription revenue."

**In sum:** The 9.8x gap reflects a **mature, ad-optimized platform versus an early-stage, investment-heavy AI-plus-social platform** that has not yet meaningfully scaled its advertising monetization or achieved operating profitability.

---

## 4. Two Structural Reasons the Ratio Cannot Be a Straightforward Monetization Efficiency Comparison

### Reason 1: Revenue Mix Incomparability (Advertising vs. Multi-Stream)

The two businesses generate revenue from fundamentally different mixes, making a single "revenue per user" ratio misleading:

| | Meta (FoA) | SpaceX (AI Segment) |
|--|-----------|---------------------|
| Advertising as % of revenue | **98.7%** | **57.6%** |
| Subscription / licensing / other | 1.3% | 42.4% |

Meta's $57.03 per-user figure is almost entirely driven by **targeted advertising**—a revenue model that scales with ad impressions, targeting precision, and advertiser demand, and that has been optimized over 20 years. SpaceX AI's $5.82 per-user figure blends advertising ($3.35) with subscription and licensing revenue ($2.47). The subscription component (X Premium, Grok subscriptions) and data licensing revenue are economically distinct from advertising: they involve recurring payments from a smaller subset of users, enterprise contracts, and API pricing that do not scale with user count the way CPM-based advertising does. Comparing the blended $5.82 to Meta's ad-dominated $57.03 conflates two fundamentally different monetization architectures. Even if SpaceX AI's advertising achieved Meta-level CPMs, its per-user figure would still appear lower because nearly half its revenue comes from non-advertising sources with structurally lower per-MAU economics.

### Reason 2: Metric Definition Mismatch — DAP vs. MAU (Disclosure Design Asymmetry)

The comparison uses **Daily Active People (DAP)** for Meta but **Monthly Active Users (MAU)** for SpaceX, and these denominators are not equivalent:

- **Meta reports DAP (3.58 billion):** Users active on at least one day during December 2025.
- **SpaceX reports MAU (550 million):** Users who interacted with Grok or X at least once in a 30-day trailing period as of March 31, 2026.

DAP is inherently a **smaller** number than MAU for the same platform, because MAU counts all users active over a 30-day window (capturing less-frequent users), while DAP counts only those active on a single representative day. If SpaceX had reported a DAP figure, it would be substantially lower than 550M (likely in the range of 200-350M based on typical DAU/MAU ratios of 0.4–0.65 for social platforms). Conversely, if Meta had reported MAU, its denominator would be substantially larger than 3.58 billion (potentially 4-5+ billion), which would **reduce** its per-user figure.

The filing design choices are material:
- Meta's deliberate shift to DAP (from the MAU it historically reported) maximizes the denominator precision and reflects engagement intensity, but produces a smaller denominator that inflates per-user metrics.
- SpaceX's disclosure of MAU rather than DAP captures a broader user base but produces a larger denominator that deflates per-user metrics.

**Net effect:** Meta's denominator is artificially compressed (smaller) relative to what an MAU figure would show, while SpaceX's denominator is artificially expanded (larger) relative to a DAP figure. Both biases work in the same direction—inflating the apparent gap. A properly normalized comparison (e.g., both on a monthly or daily basis) would still show Meta substantially ahead, but the 9.8x ratio overstates the true monetization gap by an indeterminate but likely material amount.

---

## Summary Table

| Metric | Meta (FoA) | SpaceX (AI Segment) | Ratio |
|--------|-----------|---------------------|-------|
| FY2025 Revenue | $198,759M | $3,201M | 62.1x |
| Active user metric | ~3,486M DAP | 550M MAU | — |
| Revenue per active user (annual) | **$57.03** | **$5.82** | **9.8x** |
| Advertising % of revenue | 98.7% | 57.6% | — |
| Operating income | ~$90B+ (FoA) | $(6,355)M | — |

---

## Sources

| Source | URL |
|--------|-----|
| SpaceX S-1 Filing (May 20, 2026) | https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm |
| Meta Platforms 10-K (FY2025, filed January 29, 2026) | https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1326801/000162828026003942/meta-20251231.htm |

# Meta vs. SpaceX AI Segment: Annual Revenue per Active User Comparison

## 1. Source Data

### Meta Platforms — FY2025 (from 10-K filed January 29, 2026)

| Metric | Value | Source |
|--------|-------|--------|
| Family of Apps segment revenue (FY2025) | **$198,759 million** | Segment reporting table |
| Advertising revenue (FoA) | $196,175 million (98.7% of FoA) | Revenue disaggregation |
| Other revenue (FoA) | $2,584 million (1.3% of FoA) | Revenue disaggregation |
| Annual ARPP (FY2025) | **$57.03** | Key metrics disclosure |
| Family DAP (December 2025 average) | **3.58 billion** | Key metrics disclosure |
| Implied average DAP for FY2025 | ~3,486 million | $198,759M ÷ $57.03 |

**Critical disclosure note:** Meta does **not** report Monthly Active Users (MAU) or Monthly Active People (MAP). The filing exclusively discloses **Daily Active People (DAP)**. The annual ARPP of $57.03 represents FoA revenue divided by average DAP, calculated as the sum of quarterly ARPP figures for 2025.

### SpaceX — AI Segment FY2025 (from S-1 filed May 20, 2026)

| Metric | Value | Source |
|--------|-------|--------|
| AI segment total revenue (FY2025) | **$3,201 million** | Segment disaggregation table |
| Advertising revenue (AI segment) | $1,844 million (57.6%) | Revenue disaggregation |
| AI Solutions & Infrastructure revenue | $1,357 million (42.4%) | Revenue disaggregation |
| Combined X + Grok MAU (March 31, 2026) | **550 million** | Operating metrics disclosure |
| Revenue per MAU | **$5.82** | $3,201M ÷ 550M |

---

## 2. Revenue-per-Active-User Calculations

### Meta: Implied Annual Revenue per Daily Active Person
$$\frac{\$198{,}759\text{M}}{3{,}486\text{M average DAP}} = \$57.03 \text{ per DAP (annualized)}$$

This is confirmed by the filing's own disclosed annual ARPP of **$57.03**.

### SpaceX AI Segment: Annual Revenue per MAU
$$\frac{\$3{,}201\text{M}}{550\text{M MAU}} = \$5.82 \text{ per MAU}$$

Breaking down by revenue stream:
- **Advertising revenue per MAU:** $1,844M ÷ 550M = **$3.35**
- **AI Solutions & Infrastructure per MAU:** $1,357M ÷ 550M = **$2.47**

### Ratio
$$\frac{\$57.03}{\$5.82} \approx \textbf{9.8x}$$

Meta's implied annual revenue per active user is approximately **9.8 times** that of SpaceX's AI segment.

## 3. What the Gap Implies About Monetization Maturity

- **SpaceX AI is in "investment-first" mode:** The segment posted a **$(6,355) million operating loss** in FY2025, explicitly attributed to "earlier stage of development and continued investments to support long-term growth opportunities in AI." Capital expenditures of $12,727 million signal the company is prioritizing infrastructure build-out over monetization efficiency.

- **Revenue mix is diversified, not ad-optimized:** Only 57.6% of SpaceX AI revenue comes from advertising. The remaining 42.4% ($1,357M) comes from subscriptions, data licensing, and API access—revenue streams that are inherently lower-revenue-per-user than targeted advertising at Meta's scale.

- **Growth trajectory favors the startup:** SpaceX AI's AI Solutions & Infrastructure revenue grew $465M YoY (vs. $116M growth in advertising), suggesting the company is actively shifting toward recurring, subscription-based revenue. Q1 2026 AI segment revenue of $818M (+12.5% YoY) was "primarily due to an increase in Grok and X subscription revenue."

**In sum:** The 9.8x gap reflects a **mature, ad-optimized platform versus an early-stage, investment-heavy AI-plus-social platform** that has not yet meaningfully scaled its advertising monetization or achieved operating profitability.

---

## 4. Two Structural Reasons the Ratio Cannot Be a Straightforward Monetization Efficiency Comparison

### Reason 1: Revenue Mix Incomparability (Advertising vs. Multi-Stream)

The two businesses generate revenue from fundamentally different mixes, making a single "revenue per user" ratio misleading:

| | Meta (FoA) | SpaceX (AI Segment) |
|--|-----------|---------------------|
| Advertising as % of revenue | **98.7%** | **57.6%** |
| Subscription / licensing / other | 1.3% | 42.4% |

Meta's $57.03 per-user figure is almost entirely driven by **targeted advertising**—a revenue model that scales with ad impressions, targeting precision, and advertiser demand, and that has been optimized over 20 years. SpaceX AI's $5.82 per-user figure blends advertising ($3.35) with subscription and licensing revenue ($2.47). The subscription component (X Premium, Grok subscriptions) and data licensing revenue are economically distinct from advertising: they involve recurring payments from a smaller subset of users, enterprise contracts, and API pricing that do not scale with user count the way CPM-based advertising does. Comparing the blended $5.82 to Meta's ad-dominated $57.03 conflates two fundamentally different monetization architectures. Even if SpaceX AI's advertising achieved Meta-level CPMs, its per-user figure would still appear lower because nearly half its revenue comes from non-advertising sources with structurally lower per-MAU economics.

### Reason 2: Metric Definition Mismatch — DAP vs. MAU (Disclosure Design Asymmetry)

The comparison uses **Daily Active People (DAP)** for Meta but **Monthly Active Users (MAU)** for SpaceX, and these denominators are not equivalent:

- **Meta reports DAP (3.58 billion):** Users active on at least one day during December 2025.
- **SpaceX reports MAU (550 million):** Users who interacted with Grok or X at least once in a 30-day trailing period as of March 31, 2026.

Conversely, if Meta had reported MAU, its denominator would be substantially larger than 3.58 billion (potentially 4-5+ billion), which would **reduce** its per-user figure.

- SpaceX's disclosure of MAU rather than DAP captures a broader user base but produces a larger denominator that deflates per-user metrics.

Both biases work in the same direction—inflating the apparent gap. A properly normalized comparison (e.g., both on a monthly or daily basis) would still show Meta substantially ahead, but the 9.8x ratio overstates the true monetization gap by an indeterminate but likely material amount.

---

## Summary Table

| Metric | Meta (FoA) | SpaceX (AI Segment) | Ratio |
|--------|-----------|---------------------|-------|
| FY2025 Revenue | $198,759M | $3,201M | 62.1x |
| Active user metric | ~3,486M DAP | 550M MAU | — |
